result:
The first thing that happens when I open the blank page is the hush—a cathedral-quiet that feels almost like guilt, as though every second I don’t fill it with letters is a small betrayal of possibility. Yet the hush is also mercy: no one else is watching the cursor blink its metronomic heartbeat except me, and whatever I decide to spill will stay spilled, a puddle that refuses to reflect the same sky twice.  

I think about how 1000 words is roughly the length of the gusty letter I wrote at nineteen to the stranger I was convinced I would marry. I printed it on cream paper, spritzed it with the cedar perfume that turned my skin orange along the wrists, and then—because stamps felt too irrevocable—folded it into the back pocket of a pair of jeans I never wore again. Somewhere in a landfill the ink is still migrating, letter by letter, into the groundwater of an unmapped town.  

Last week at the thrift store I found a child’s plaster handprint painted gold. The name “LUKE” was carved underneath, already flecked with the kind of chips that suggest storied summers in a sandbox. I carried it around the aisles like a votive, imagining Luke at thirty-five, maybe stubbled, maybe piloting a commuter train, having no idea that the negative space of his five-year-old palm is cupped by a stranger who almost bought his artefact for $2.49. I set it back beside a cracked Blue’s Clues mug because I suddenly felt like a trespasser, because I remembered that memories not yours can still cut your tongue if you try to swallow them whole.  

I read somewhere that the universe is flat as a dinner plate within a margin of error the width of a hydrogen atom. This means that if you set out in a straight line you’d come back to where you started, only everything would be older, and you might arrive to find yourself already waiting, annoyed that you’re late to a rendezvous neither version of you precisely scheduled.  

When I was eight I believed the earth’s core was an enormous apricot. I told my best friend Jonah and he punched me in the stomach—not out of malice, but of theological panic, as though my heresy might cause the planet to bite down on its own pit and implode in a sticky cosmic jam. We lay on the driveway afterwards counting contrails, pretending each plane was a bead on the abacus of distance, clicking our tongues every time one dissolved into the blue that proved nothing beyond the fact that blue is patient.  

There are 168 hours in a week, which translates to 10,080 minutes, which feels like a lie when you’re sitting in the fluoresced lobby of a hospital at 3:12 a.m. waiting to discover whether the word “stable” has been promoted from adjective to benediction. I have learned that coffee purchased from vending machines tastes of nickel and of every other sleepless hand that has pressed the same sticky button. I have learned that the distance between floor tiles is exactly one unfinished prayer.  

The average cloud, meteorologists insist, weighs about as much as 100 elephants. Yet we trust them to wander overhead like aimless Roman gods, never once demanding they file for insurance against the possibility of falling. Perhaps this is why we find it so easy to ignore looming disasters: practice.  

My grandfather stored bullets in an old shortbread tin. When he shook it, the brass sang like primitive bells. He told me each cartridge was a minute he’d borrowed from death and would someday have to return with interest. When he passed, the funeral director asked the family if we wanted him buried with “items of significance.” I slipped in one bullet, because ritual needs ballast, and because I liked the thought of him negotiating terms with the underworld in a language that clinked.  

I keep trying to write about the sound of snow but I always end up writing about the sound of absence: how a bootprint is a doorway you can never close, how the air after a flurry holds its breath the way a theater does after the last line of a tragedy, waiting for applause that might just be coughing.  

The problem with promises is they are always written in future tense, a grammatical country no border patrol can secure. Yet we keep issuing them like IOUs against the minutes we haven’t met, convinced that tomorrow will honor our signature even as the ink smudges under the first drip of now.  

Once, on a bus in Serbia, a woman handed me half an orange without a word. The rind was cold from the window’s frost, and when I dug my thumb into the peel it sighed the way an old book does when opened after centuries shut. The segments were pale, almost lunar, and tasted of someone else’s summer. I have never forgotten that silent generosity, the way the orange’s other half continued traveling toward a destination I will never know, like the twin of a meteor that burnt up above an ocean it never learned to pronounce.  

We speak of “finding” time the way we speak of “finding” lost keys, as though chronology is always slipping between couch cushions rather than evaporating under the friction of our touching. Yet every moment is right here, camped inside the same cubic inch of present, waving frantic little flags that look—from the outside—identical to stillness.  

Here is what I want to do with my 1000 words: I want to tuck a small corner of blanket over the shoulder of everyone who has ever googled “how to disappear” at 2 a.m. I want to tell them disappearance is just another country, one that stamps your passport in invisible ink and makes you carry your luggage on your chest like a lead apron. I want to say that staying is also a kind of flight—into the vast interior where the runway lights are the eyes of people who refuse to look away.  

I want to confess that I still don’t know how to end things. Essays, relationships, the tiny plastic clasp on a bag of carrots—everything either surrenders too easily or clings until I’m using teeth and kitchen shears and muttering curses that sound like avant-garde lullabies.  

Years ago a man on the metro told me the secret to happiness was “never turn off a movie before the bus explodes.” I have followed this advice literally and figuratively, stuck through narratives that dragged like wet denim only to discover the detonation was metaphor and the passengers were just ideas I had about myself, burning beautifully.  

In Japanese there is a word, “kuchisabishii,” which means “lonely mouth”—the ache that makes you eat not from hunger but from the need to place something inside the dark cathedral of your body that isn’t echo. I think of how we do that with words too: cram them into each other’s emptiness until the resonance changes key.  

I used to carry a pocket-sized-notebook titled “Evidence I Exist.” It held ticket stubs and pressed weeds and lines from songs I misheard on purpose. One day I left it on a park bench because I suddenly understood the list was endless and the only proof I needed was the heart still trying to rhyme “damage” with “language,” as if the two could be stitched into the same coat lining.  

If you walk south from my apartment for exactly 986 steps you reach a mural of a whale breaching through brick. At night the streetlamp glints off the painted water so convincingly you expect the concrete to be wet. I’ve seen drunk strangers kneel and touch it, reverent, as though they might summon tide or forgiveness or both.  

We forget that every map is a love-letter to simultaneity: here and there existing at once, refusing the tyranny of sequence. The cartographer’s pen says you can be in Paris and in pain at the same time, that latitude has room for both carnival and cardiac arrest.  

Somewhere, a child is learning the word “yesterday” and realizing it is a raft that keeps drifting further without once demanding oars. Somewhere else, an elder is forgetting the same word and discovering memory is just the echo of a shoreline after the boat has rounded the cliff.  

I want to close these 1000 words the way a violin case closes on velvet: with the instrument inside still humming, certain that lid and darkness are only temporary geographies. I want to tell you that every sentence is a door you can walk through backwards, that the knob will feel warm from the palm of whoever turns it next, and that leaving is just another way of arriving at the place you started, only now you can pronounce its fragile name.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
